This might be a regression, but I need to confirm it.
The logs seem to suggest a timeout under load.
Apparently, it only happens on the latest build.
I would say it could be worth running one more test.
Ana, are we good to ship the payment update tomorrow?
I would say we are mostly there, though there is one thing I would like to flag, tentatively.
Sure, go ahead. What is on your mind?
The logs seem to suggest an intermittent timeout under heavy load. I am being cautious because it is not confirmed yet.
So, presumably, you would rather we hold the release?
I would not put it that strongly. It could be worth running one more load test first.
Fair enough. And roughly how long would that take?
Apparently about two hours. I am fairly confident we would know for sure by the afternoon.
That sounds sensible. Let us hold it until we are sure, then.
I would say we are mostly there, but there is one risk I would like to flag.
The logs seem to suggest an intermittent issue.
I would not put it that strongly; it might be worth another test.
I am not sure that is quite right, to be honest.
Roughly speaking, it would take about two hours.

Let's talk about how you sound in a meeting when you are not one hundred percent sure. Many people think that hedging -- softening what you say -- makes you sound weak. In my experience, the opposite is true. When you say 'this might be a regression' instead of 'this is a regression', you sound more measured and more credible, because you are being honest about what you actually know. Being too blunt is the real risk, especially in an international meeting. If you say 'you are wrong' or 'that will never work', you can come across as arrogant, and you shut the conversation down. A good hedge keeps the door open. But there is a limit. One hedge per sentence is usually enough. If you pile them up -- 'I think that maybe it could possibly perhaps be a small issue' -- then you sound unsure, and people stop trusting your judgment. So aim for one clear, soft claim. Say what you think, qualify it once, and let the room respond. That is how you sound both precise and diplomatic.

Hi Ana, it's Sofia from the platform team, just a quick note before the standup. I saw your message about the timeout, and honestly, the way you phrased it was perfect. I learned to soften my messages the hard way, on my first international project, when a manager told me I sounded too direct in writing. So here is my favorite tip. Instead of 'you are wrong', which shuts people down, try 'I'm not sure that's quite right'. It says the same thing, but it keeps the conversation open. And when you're not certain, say 'the logs seem to suggest', not 'the logs prove'. It's honest, and it protects you if you turn out to be wrong. One warning, though. Don't over-soften. If you hedge every single word, nobody can tell what you actually think, and that's just as unclear as being too blunt. Say what you mean, soften it once, and stop there. You're getting really good at this. See you in the standup.

Here is how Ana raised her concern without sounding blunt. First, she checked the logs and formed a tentative view of the problem. Then, she softened her message so it would not sound like an accusation. After that, she suggested one more load test instead of demanding a delay. Next, she gave a rough estimate of how long the test would take. Finally, she let the team decide, and they agreed to hold the release.
